Examples of possible ways to organize a lecture summary
As text paragraphs:
A membrane forms the border or division between the contents of a cell or vesicle and its surroundings. Membranes are composed of a phospholipid bilayer with embedded and associated proteins. A phospholipid has a 3-carbon backbone with a charged or polar “head group” attached to one carbon and fatty acid hydrocarbon chains attached to the other two. The fatty acids form a hydrophobic cluster, while the head groups interact with the aqueous solutions inside and outside the cell.
Lipids differ in both the nature of their head groups, although they always contain a phosphate group, and in the fatty acids they incorporate. Phospholipids with saturated hydrocarbon chains will pack close together, while those with unsaturated fatty acids will have small gaps between them. In animal cells only, those gaps can be filled by cholesterol. Membranes in which a large fraction of the phospholipids have unsaturated fatty acid tails will be more fluid than those with primarily saturated fatty acid tails – this is important if the cell needs to be able to change shape easily, such as in ameboid movement. It’s also easier for molecules to move through the membrane by diffusion if the phospholipid tails are less tightly packed. Phospholipids can move within their layer, but very rarely change layers.
Integral membrane proteins are embedded in the membranes; hydrophobic amino acid side groups cluster with the fatty acid tails and polar or charged side groups form the intracellular and extracellular domains.  Peripheral membrane proteins can be inside or outside the cell. They associate with the polar head groups by non-covalent interactions (primarily hydrogen bonds), but are not embedded in the hydrophobic portion of the bilayer. Proteins also change their relative position in or adjacent to a membrane, but once situated seldom move from one side of a membrane to the other.

Examples of possible ways to organize a lecture summary
In outline form:
I. Membrane structure
A. Phospholipids
1. 3-carbon backbone
2. polar or charged “head group” – phosphate
3. fatty acid “tails”
i. saturated = more rigid membrane
ii. unsaturated = more fluid
- cell motility/shape change
- diffusion of molecules
- cholesterol - animal cells only
4. movement of phospholipids
i. move relative to others in same layer easily
ii. rarely flip from one layer to the other
B. Proteins
1. integral = embedded in membrane
i. hydrophobic regions of protein cluster w/fatty acids
ii. polar/charged amino acids = intracellular, extracellular domains
2. peripheral = associated with one side, not embedded
i. mostly H-bonds between side groups and polar head groups
3. move around, but don't cross membrane

Examples of possible ways to organize a lecture summary
As a bullet point list:
 Membrane – phospholipid bilayer + proteins
 Phospholipid = polar/charged group with phosphate, two fatty acid chains
 Polar head group - interacts with aqueous solutions inside, outside
 Fatty acid tails - saturated or unsaturated
membrane fluidity, cell shape change/motility, diffusion of molecules
cholesterol in animal cells - gaps between unsaturated fatty acids
 Phospholipids move, don't change layers
 Proteins - integral or peripheral
integral - embedded in or span membrane
peripheral - associate with polar head groups on one side
float on/in bilayer, don't cross membrane
Any format that works for you is fine – the goals are:
identify key points
practice using terms, explaining things in your own words
spend time working with the information

Molecular Biology
study of life at the molecular level
DNA replication, mutations and repair mechanisms
transcription, RNA processing, translation
control of gene expression
set of techniques for manipulating molecules
gene cloning, DNA sequencing
manipulation of genes, modifying proteins
transgenic organisms
Everybody uses molecular biology 

1952 – Watson and Crick, double helix
Nobel Prize, 1962
1968/1970 – first restriction enzymes
1980s – transgenic organisms
polymerase chain reaction (PCR)
1990-2003 – human genome project
high-throughput sequencing
2012 – CRISPR/Cas9 genome editing

molecular biology focuses on nucleic acids
DNA replication - mechanisms, regulation
mutations and repair mechanisms
transcription and RNA processing
translation
regulation of gene expression
biochemistry focuses on proteins
structure-function relationships
mechanisms of catalysis - biologically important reactions
protein-protein interactions
genetics - focuses on mechanisms and patterns of inheritance
chromosome and genome structure and organization
gene interactions/genetic networks
population genetics - allele frequencies, etc

molecular biology focuses on nucleic acids
DNA replication - mechanisms, regulation
mutations and repair mechanisms
transcription and RNA processing
translation
regulation of gene expression
biochemistry focuses on proteins
genetics - focuses on mechanisms and patterns of inheritance
cell biology also focuses more on proteins, at cellular level
protein processing, targeting and transport
cell morphology and movement - cytoskeleton
membrane structure and function, transport across membranes
cell signaling and signal transduction
cell adhesion and cell-ECM interactions
cell cycle control

Course infrastructure
Tips for success
Overview of molecular biology as a discipline
Logic of molecular biology, model systems
the only content you are responsible for

The Logic of Molecular Biology
Be reductionist
Expect efficiency
Quantitative analysis
Use model systems - nature is conservative
Develop and test conceptual models -
infer accuracy of model from lack of contradiction

Properties of a good model system:
1 – simplicity, ability to isolate process of interest
2 – easy to grow or culture
3 – short generation time
Typical model systems in molecular biology:
Bacteriophage and viruses
Bacteria – especially E. coli
Yeasts
Cultured animal and plant cells
Transgenic organisms (Arabidopsis, mouse, zebrafish)
